title: USS Roosevelt Encounters
subtitle: GIMBAL and GOFAST
location: US East Coast — W-72 Operating Area, off Virginia/North Carolina
heroImageAlt: East Coast OPAREA map showing W-72 encounter zones

timeline.title: First anomalous radar contacts detected
timeline.detail: Pilots from Carrier Air Wing 8 aboard USS Theodore Roosevelt begin detecting anomalous objects on radar and ATFLIR systems during training exercises in the W-72 warning area off the Virginia/North Carolina coast.

timeline.title: Encounters become daily occurrence
timeline.detail: Lt. Ryan Graves (VFA-11) and other pilots report near-daily detection of objects on radar and SA displays. Objects appear stationary at 30,000 ft or moving at high speed with no visible propulsion.

timeline.title: Lt. Accoin detects objects on radar and IR simultaneously
timeline.detail: Lt. Danny Accoin (VFA-31) reports radar lock on objects that are invisible to the naked eye. ATFLIR pod confirms same contacts. Multi-sensor detection rules out single-system malfunction.

timeline.title: GIMBAL video captured
timeline.detail: F/A-18 aircrew capture the GIMBAL infrared video showing a dark, oblong object that appears to rotate against its direction of flight. Pilot audio expresses astonishment and references 'a whole fleet of them' on instruments. No exhaust signature detected.

timeline.title: GOFAST video captured
timeline.detail: Separate ATFLIR recording captures a small, apparently spherical object moving at high speed near the ocean surface against strong winds. No thermal exhaust signature. Object appears to have no visible means of propulsion.

timeline.title: Near-miss incident reported
timeline.detail: A VFA-11 pilot comes within meters of an unidentified object during a training sortie. The encounter is filed as a formal safety hazard report through Navy channels. The object nearly struck the aircraft.

timeline.title: Roosevelt deploys to Persian Gulf
timeline.detail: USS Theodore Roosevelt departs for deployment to the Persian Gulf. Encounters in the W-72 area cease for the deployed squadrons, though it is unknown whether other units subsequently reported similar contacts in the area.

timeline.title: GIMBAL and GOFAST videos surface publicly
timeline.detail: The videos are released alongside the New York Times investigation into AATIP. To The Stars Academy publishes the footage. Pentagon acknowledges the videos are authentic Department of Defense material.

timeline.title: DoD officially declassifies all three videos
timeline.detail: The Department of Defense formally releases GIMBAL, GOFAST, and FLIR1, confirming they are authentic, unclassified Navy videos. DoD states the release is to 'clear up any misconceptions' about whether the footage is real.

timeline.title: Lt. Ryan Graves testifies before Congress
timeline.detail: Graves testifies under oath before the House Oversight Committee alongside CDR David Fravor and David Grusch. He describes daily encounters, the near-miss incident, and the stigma pilots face when reporting UAP. His testimony helps establish the frequency and persistence of East Coast encounters.

evidence.title: GIMBAL Infrared Video
evidence.summary: ~34 seconds of ATFLIR footage captured January 21, 2015. Object appears to rotate against direction of flight. No exhaust plume. Pilot audio references 'a whole fleet of them.' Officially declassified by DoD April 2020.
evidence.linkText: Linked to: GOFAST Video, Graves Testimony, ATFLIR Data
evidence.imageAlt: GIMBAL infrared still showing rotating object

evidence.title: GOFAST Infrared Video
evidence.summary: ~34 seconds of ATFLIR footage from January 2015. Small object moves at apparent high speed near ocean surface against strong winds. No thermal signature, no visible propulsion. Officially declassified by DoD April 2020.
evidence.linkText: Linked to: GIMBAL Video, Graves Testimony, Speed Analysis
evidence.imageAlt: GOFAST infrared still showing high-speed object near ocean surface

evidence.title: Lt. Graves Congressional Testimony
evidence.summary: Sworn testimony before House Oversight Committee, July 26, 2023. Graves testified encounters were 'every day for at least a couple years.' Described near-miss incident, objects with no infrared signature hovering at 30,000 ft.
evidence.linkText: Linked to: GIMBAL Video, Near-Miss Report, Daily Encounters

evidence.title: Multi-Sensor Correlation Data
evidence.summary: AN/APG-79 AESA radar and ATFLIR pod independently detected the same objects. Lt. Accoin reported radar lock on contacts invisible to the naked eye. Multi-platform confirmation rules out single-system error.
evidence.linkText: Linked to: Accoin Testimony, GIMBAL, GOFAST

evidence.title: Near-Miss Safety Report
evidence.summary: Formal safety hazard report filed through Navy channels after a VFA-11 pilot came within meters of an unidentified object. This incident contributed to the Navy establishing formal UAP reporting procedures in 2019.
evidence.linkText: Linked to: Graves Testimony, Navy Reporting Guidelines

witnesses.name: Lt. Ryan Graves
witnesses.observed: Near-daily encounters over approximately two years. Objects stationary at 30,000 ft with no visible propulsion or IR signature. Reported near-miss incident. First active-duty pilot to testify before Congress on UAP.
witnesses.quote: “Every day for at least a couple years.”

witnesses.name: Lt. Danny Accoin
witnesses.observed: Detected objects simultaneously on AN/APG-79 AESA radar and ATFLIR infrared pod. Objects were invisible to the naked eye despite confirmed sensor contacts at close range. Multi-sensor correlation ruled out single-system malfunction.
witnesses.quote: “I almost hit one of those things.”

witnesses.name: VFA-11 / VFA-31 Pilots
witnesses.observed: Multiple pilots across Carrier Air Wing 8 independently reported encounters with unidentified objects. Encounters were so routine they became accepted hazards during training sorties in the W-72 area.

witnesses.name: ATFLIR Operators
witnesses.observed: WSOs operating the AN/ASQ-228 ATFLIR pods captured the GIMBAL and GOFAST footage. Their sensor expertise confirmed the contacts were real objects tracked by calibrated military-grade infrared systems.

aiAnalysis.title: Cross-Case: Rotation Behavior Pattern
aiAnalysis.finding: The GIMBAL object's apparent rotation matches a behavior pattern seen in the Nimitz FLIR1 video (2004), where the Tic Tac appeared to rotate on its longitudinal axis. Both events involve ATFLIR captures of objects demonstrating rotation without corresponding trajectory change — a flight characteristic inconsistent with any known aircraft.
aiAnalysis.confidence: Medium — GIMBAL rotation could be pod artifact

aiAnalysis.title: Duration Anomaly: 2-Year Encounter Pattern
aiAnalysis.finding: The ~2-year duration of daily encounters is unprecedented in high-credibility UAP cases. Most cases involve hours or days. Sustained daily presence implies either a persistent surveillance platform, a recurring natural phenomenon, or a fixed sensor anomaly. The variety of sensor confirmations (radar, IR, occasional visual) argues against a purely instrumental explanation.

aiAnalysis.title: Military Operating Area Concentration
aiAnalysis.finding: Both the Nimitz (W-291, SOCAL) and Roosevelt (W-72, East Coast) encounters occurred in designated military warning areas used for carrier training exercises. This could indicate either heightened observation capability in these areas (more sensors) or that UAP activity correlates with military operations — a pattern noted by the DNI preliminary assessment (June 2021).
aiAnalysis.confidence: Medium — correlation does not imply causation

openQuestions.text: Is the GIMBAL rotation the actual object rotating, or an artifact of the ATFLIR gimbal mechanism?
openQuestions.context: Skeptic Mick West has argued the rotation is consistent with the ATFLIR pod's derotation algorithm. Multiple defense analysts dispute this interpretation. Resolution requires access to the raw sensor data and pod engineering specifications.

openQuestions.text: Why did encounters occur daily for approximately two years?
openQuestions.context: This sustained pattern is unprecedented. It implies either a fixed phenomenon in the W-72 area, a persistent surveillance platform, or a recurring sensor artifact — each with very different implications.

openQuestions.text: What was the true speed of the GOFAST object?
openQuestions.context: Parallax effects from the tracking aircraft's altitude and airspeed complicate speed calculations. Some analyses suggest the object may be slower than it appears, while others confirm anomalous velocity.

openQuestions.text: Are there additional classified videos from the Roosevelt encounters?
openQuestions.context: Graves and other pilots reported daily encounters over two years. Only two short videos have been declassified. The volume of potential sensor data is enormous.

openQuestions.text: Did the encounters continue in the W-72 area after Roosevelt deployed?
openQuestions.context: It is unknown whether subsequent units training in the same area reported similar contacts. This could help determine whether the phenomenon was tied to the location or the carrier strike group.

resolution.label: Sensor glitch / ATFLIR artifact
resolution.detail: Partially addressed. GIMBAL rotation specifically debated — could be pod derotation artifact. However, multi-sensor confirmation (radar + IR + occasional visual) across two years makes systemic sensor error unlikely for all encounters.

resolution.label: Classified drone program
resolution.detail: Not eliminated. Objects' persistent presence in a military operating area is consistent with surveillance drone behavior. However, no known drone matches the observed flight characteristics (stationary at 30,000 ft, no IR signature).

resolution.label: Atmospheric phenomena
resolution.detail: Eliminated for primary encounters. Objects tracked on calibrated military radar and IR simultaneously. Atmospheric effects do not produce coherent multi-sensor returns over extended periods.

resolution.label: Bird or balloon
resolution.detail: Eliminated. Objects detected at 30,000 ft altitude, stationary in high winds, with no thermal signature. No bird or balloon matches these characteristics. Near-miss incident confirms solid object.

Obtain ATFLIR AN/ASQ-228 engineering specs to resolve GIMBAL rotation debate
Cross-reference FAA and NUFORC databases for civilian reports near W-72 (2014–2015)
Interview additional CVW-8 pilots who have not spoken publicly

verifiedFacts.fact: GIMBAL and GOFAST videos were officially declassified by the U.S. Department of Defense on April 27, 2020.
Lt. Ryan Graves testified under oath before the House Oversight Committee on July 26, 2023.
sources: U.S. House Committee on Oversight and Accountability — official record

verifiedFacts.fact: Graves stated under oath that encounters were 'every day for at least a couple years.'
A near-miss incident occurred where a pilot came within meters of an unidentified object during a training sortie.
sources: Formal Navy safety hazard report filed (content classified)

verifiedFacts.fact: Objects were detected loitering at approximately 30,000 feet altitude with no visible means of propulsion.
sources: Lt. Ryan Graves — multiple interviews and congressional testimony

verifiedFacts.fact: The GIMBAL object appears to rotate against its direction of flight in the ATFLIR footage.
sources: GIMBAL video — direct observation of footage
verifiedFacts.disputedBy: Mick West and others argue the rotation may be an artifact of the ATFLIR pod's derotation mechanism rather than true object rotation. Defense analysts dispute this interpretation.

verifiedFacts.fact: Objects displayed no infrared heat signature despite being detected on military-grade infrared sensors.
sources: GIMBAL and GOFAST ATFLIR footage — no exhaust plume visible
Lt. Graves testimony — specifically noted absence of IR signature
Lt. Accoin — confirmed IR detection without thermal exhaust

hypothesisProbabilities.reasoning: While the GIMBAL rotation specifically could be an ATFLIR pod artifact, the broader pattern of daily encounters across multiple sensor systems (AESA radar, ATFLIR IR, occasional visual) over two years makes systemic sensor error highly unlikely. The near-miss incident confirms at least some objects were physically present.

The persistent presence in a military operating area is consistent with a surveillance platform. However, no known drone can hover at 30,000 ft without propulsion, produce zero IR signature, and nearly collide with military jets without any operational deconfliction. If classified, the near-miss should have triggered immediate deconfliction through military channels.

Multi-sensor detection (radar + IR simultaneously) and the near-miss with a physical object eliminate atmospheric phenomena for the primary encounters. Atmospheric effects do not produce coherent radar and infrared returns at 30,000 ft for extended periods.

The only hypothesis consistent with all evidence: multi-sensor detection, physical near-miss, sustained daily presence, no IR exhaust, and stationary hover at altitude. The combination of flight characteristics exceeds any known technology. Congressional testimony under oath adds significant weight to the witness accounts.

researchBranches.question: Is GIMBAL rotation the object or the pod mechanism?
researchBranches.anchorText: The GIMBAL object appears to rotate against its direction of flight in the ATFLIR footage.

findings.content: Mick West argues the rotation matches the AN/ASQ-228 ATFLIR pod's derotation algorithm — as the pod gimbal reaches a mechanical limit, the entire image rotates to maintain tracking. This would mean the 'rotation' is the background rotating, not the object.

Defense analyst Dave Beaty and others argue that the object's shape visibly changes during the rotation in a way inconsistent with a simple image rotation. The object's IR signature also appears to shift, suggesting true physical rotation rather than a sensor artifact.

Resolution requires access to the raw ATFLIR sensor data and Raytheon AN/ASQ-228 engineering documentation — specifically the derotation algorithm behavior at gimbal limits. This data is likely classified or proprietary.

researchBranches.question: Why daily encounters for 2 years — what does sustained behavior imply?
researchBranches.anchorText: Graves stated under oath that encounters were 'every day for at least a couple years.'

findings.content: Three competing interpretations: (1) Persistent surveillance platform — implies intent and capability to maintain extended presence, possibly monitoring carrier strike group operations. (2) Recurring natural phenomenon tied to the W-72 geographic area — would require identification of a mechanism. (3) Sensor artifact specific to CVW-8 aircraft or the W-72 electromagnetic environment — contradicted by multi-sensor and occasional visual confirmation.

The DNI Preliminary Assessment (June 2021) noted that UAP reports clustered around military training areas and ranges. This could reflect either genuine UAP interest in military operations or simply higher sensor density and observer awareness in these areas.
findings.source: Office of the Director of National Intelligence — Preliminary Assessment: UAP (June 25, 2021)
